knowledges.content: Test-Time Scaling (TTS) for LLM reasoning includes parallel methods (e.g., Best-of-N) and sequential refinement, both of which depend on a reward model (verifier) to discern solution quality and guide search.
knowledges.label: tts_setting

knowledges.content: Recent milestones on the International Mathematical Olympiad by OpenAI and Google highlight the rapid ascent of reasoning models such as Gemini-3 and DeepSeekMath-V2.
knowledges.label: reasoning_milestones_setting

knowledges.content: The efficacy of Test-Time Scaling for LLM reasoning is fundamentally dependent on the reward model (verifier) used to score and select candidate solutions.
knowledges.label: verifier_role_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Verifier centrality to TTS is asserted by multiple cited works (Gui 2024; Kang 2025) and is the foundational premise of the entire TTS literature.

knowledges.content: Outcome-level RMs (ORMs) and process-level RMs (PRMs) only emit scalar values and therefore lack interpretability when used as verifiers.
knowledges.label: orm_prm_limitation_claim

knowledges.content: Generative Reward Models (GenRMs) trained with next-token prediction typically employ single-turn reasoning to assess candidate solutions.
knowledges.label: genrm_single_turn_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Single-turn assessment is a definitional / observational property of the GenRM paradigm as the authors and the cited works describe it.

knowledges.content: Single-turn GenRMs suffer from error propagation: because LLMs are mostly trained on correct or near-correct solutions, they struggle to issue a correct verdict when conditioned on a flawed solution and are easily misled by superficially plausible but incorrect answers.
knowledges.label: error_propagation_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Supported by Zhang et al. 2025b and illustrated by the paper's Figure 1 case study; an established failure mode of single-turn LLM verifiers.

knowledges.content: Single-turn GenRMs lack external grounding: without integration with symbolic solvers or external tools, they hallucinate on computation-intensive or knowledge-heavy tasks and exhibit unstable performance.
knowledges.label: external_grounding_gap_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Well-attested phenomenon for tool-free LLM verifiers (Dong 2025a/b; Feng 2025); consistent with the broader hallucination literature.

knowledges.content: Existing GenRMs are unreliable in complex domains because they suffer from error propagation and lack external grounding.
knowledges.label: genrm_unreliable_claim

knowledges.content: Reliable verification calls for an agentic reward modeling paradigm that uses multi-turn reasoning integrated with external tools.
knowledges.label: paradigm_shift_claim

knowledges.content: Reward Models (RMs) play a pivotal role in aligning large language models with human preferences. Outcome-level RMs assign scalar rewards to complete responses; PRMs supervise intermediate steps; GenRMs reformulate reward modeling as next-token prediction.
knowledges.label: rm_alignment_setting

knowledges.content: Existing tool-augmented and LLM-as-Judge reward models either fail to tightly integrate tool execution into the reasoning process or do not provide point-wise feedback required for test-time scaling.
knowledges.label: prior_tool_augmented_rm_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Author's own characterization of the prior tool-augmented RM literature, with citations (Li 2024; Xu 2025; Peng 2025); plausible but partly polemical.

knowledges.content: Reformulating verification as a bidirectional, multi-turn, tool-augmented process is novel relative to prior reward-model and TTS literature.
knowledges.label: bidirectional_novelty_claim

knowledges.content: Parallel Scaling (Best-of-N): for input x, the actor samples k candidate solutions {y(j)}; the verifier scores each candidate via the True-token logit of its verifying rationale and selects the highest-confidence one.
knowledges.label: bon_setting

knowledges.content: Sequential Scaling: given query x and an initial solution y0, the verifier produces a critique f0; the actor then refines its solution iteratively, y_t ~ pi_theta(x, y0, f0, ..., y_{t-1}, f_{t-1}).
knowledges.label: sequential_setting

knowledges.content: Forward agent: starting from problem premises, sequentially traces the solution path via a Plan-Validate-Verdict pipeline, validating that each step v_{i-1} is a sufficient condition for v_i. Empowered to invoke a Python interpreter and to interleave Thought-Action-Observation rounds.
knowledges.label: forward_agent_setting

knowledges.content: Backward agent: reasons in reverse from the final answer back to the problem statement via the same Plan-Validate-Verdict pipeline, checking that all required problem constraints are fulfilled (necessity check).
knowledges.label: backward_agent_setting

knowledges.content: AgentV-RL training recipe: synthetic data engine that role-plays forward and backward agents to generate verification trajectories with quality control, followed by a two-stage schema of rejection-sampling SFT and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning. The verifier is trained on 15K SFT and 50K RL samples starting from Qwen3-4B.
knowledges.label: agentv_setting

knowledges.content: GRPO objective uses a clipped importance ratio with a KL-to-reference penalty and filters zero-variance reward groups; binary outcome reward r=1 if predicted verdict matches ground truth, else -1; compiler execution observations are excluded from the loss to mitigate environment memorization.
knowledges.label: grpo_setting

knowledges.content: The forward agent performs sufficiency checking by validating that each preceding step is a sufficient condition for the subsequent derivation.
knowledges.label: forward_sufficiency_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Definitional description of the forward agent, supported by the explicit Plan-Validate-Verdict template in Section 3.2 and the Figure 3 case study.

knowledges.content: The backward agent performs necessity checking by validating that the solution is grounded in the original problem constraints.
knowledges.label: backward_necessity_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Definitional description of the backward agent under the same Plan-Validate-Verdict template; aggregated with the forward agent in Appendix C.3.

knowledges.content: Coordinating a forward (sufficiency) agent and a backward (necessity) agent yields a bidirectional, tool-augmented review that is more comprehensive than single-turn or unidirectional verification.
knowledges.label: bidirectional_design_claim

knowledges.content: Both agents can iteratively decompose complex solutions into verifiable sub-steps and invoke external tools (e.g., a Python interpreter) for numerical calculation.
knowledges.label: tool_augmented_capability_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Tool integration is implemented and described in Appendix B.2.3; agents demonstrably invoke a Python interpreter in the documented trajectories.

knowledges.content: The capability of the multi-agent forward+backward system can be distilled into a single LLM via the AgentV-RL recipe (synthetic-data SFT followed by GRPO RL).
knowledges.label: multi_agent_distillable_claim

knowledges.content: Equation 8: J_GRPO(psi) = E[(1/G) sum_i (1/|H_i|) sum_t min(r_{i,t}*A_{i,t}, clip(r_{i,t}, 1-eps_low, 1+eps_high)*A_{i,t})] - beta * D_KL(pi_psi || pi_ref), with r_{i,t} the importance-sampling ratio; zero-variance reward groups are filtered.
knowledges.label: grpo_objective_setting

knowledges.content: Filtering zero-variance reward groups and using clipped importance ratios with a KL penalty yields a stable GRPO update for verifier training.
knowledges.label: grpo_stability_claim

knowledges.content: Best-of-N evaluation on MATH500, GSM8K, Gaokao2023, and AIME24 with N in {32, 64, 128}, comparing against text reasoning LLMs, ORMs (e.g., INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B, Skywork-V2-Llama-8B), and PRMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-7B, Math-Shepherd, Skywork-PRM).
knowledges.label: bon_benchmarks_setting

knowledges.content: Iterative refinement evaluation across three turns on the same benchmarks, reporting Acc, Delta-up (correction rate), and Delta-down (degradation rate) per turn against verifier baselines including DS-Distill-14B and Qwen3-4B-Think.
knowledges.label: iter_refine_setting

knowledges.content: On Table 1, Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves the best BoN accuracy in the Ours category across MATH500 (up to 79.0% at N=128), GSM8K (93.3%), Gaokao2023 (57.4%), and AIME24 (53.3%), surpassing the previous best ORM Skywork-V2-Llama-8B on MATH500 by 25.2 percentage points.
knowledges.label: table1_observation

metadata.prior_justification: Direct numerical read-off from Table 1 (BoN performance); near-self-certifying given the published table.

knowledges.content: On Table 2, Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B yields the highest accuracy and the largest Delta-up (e.g., 41.6% on MATH500, 40.3% on Gaokao2023 in turn 1) with low Delta-down, and remains stable across turns 2 and 3 while baselines such as DS-Distill-14B exhibit larger degradations.
knowledges.label: table2_observation

metadata.prior_justification: Direct numerical read-off from Table 2 (iterative refinement); high confidence in the reported numbers.

knowledges.content: Agentic Verifier achieves state-of-the-art Best-of-N performance across MATH500, GSM8K, Gaokao2023, and AIME24, with the 4B variant outperforming the prior best ORM (Skywork-V2-Llama-8B) on MATH500 by 25.2 percentage points.
knowledges.label: bon_sota_claim

knowledges.content: Agentic Verifier's BoN accuracy continues to improve as N grows from 32 to 128, particularly on challenging benchmarks like AIME24 (reaching 53.3% at N=128 with the 4B variant), unlike many baselines whose accuracy plateaus or declines.
knowledges.label: bon_scales_with_n_claim

knowledges.content: Agentic Verifier provides high-quality critiques that drive faster convergence in iterative refinement: high Delta-up with low Delta-down in early turns and stable or only slightly declining accuracy in later turns, avoiding the degradation seen with competing verifiers.
knowledges.label: iter_refine_helpful_claim

knowledges.content: Outcome-level reward models (in particular Skywork-V2-Llama-8B and INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B) match or exceed Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B on the four BoN benchmarks.
knowledges.label: orm_dominance_null_claim

metadata.prior_justification: Null hypothesis the paper sets out to refute: Table 1 directly contradicts ORM dominance over Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B. Low prior; the contradiction edge to bon_sota_claim further suppresses it under BP.

knowledges.content: not_both_true(A, B)
knowledges.label: orm_null_contradiction

knowledges.content: Ablation study (Figure 4) compares (a) forward-agent-only, (b) backward-agent-only, and (c) the full Agentic Verifier under both BoN sampling and verifier-revision.
knowledges.label: ablation_setting

knowledges.content: In Figure 4, both the forward-only and backward-only variants are competitive, but the full Agentic Verifier (forward + backward) achieves the best accuracy on MATH500 and Gaokao2023 across N in {32, 64, 128} and across refinement rounds.
knowledges.label: ablation_observation

metadata.prior_justification: Direct read-off from Figure 4 ablation: forward/backward variants competitive and full design strictly best on the plotted benchmarks.

knowledges.content: The forward and backward agents are synergistic: combining them yields strictly better verification performance than either single-direction variant.
knowledges.label: bidirectional_synergy_claim

knowledges.content: Tool ablations in Appendix C.5: a no-tool variant of Agentic Verifier already outperforms the base model; adding the Python tool further improves performance, with tool usage moderate and stable in practice.
knowledges.label: tool_ablation_setting

knowledges.content: Both the agentic structure and tool integration contribute additively to verifier performance, with the agentic structure providing the larger share of the gain.
knowledges.label: tool_contributes_claim

knowledges.content: Figure 5 controllable study compares Train-free, SFT, and SFT+RL variants on BoN. Train-free already beats the base model by up to 2.6 points on Gaokao2023; SFT adds further gains; SFT+RL achieves the strongest results.
knowledges.label: training_ablation_setting

knowledges.content: Each stage of the AgentV-RL recipe contributes a measurable improvement: Train-free > Base, SFT > Train-free, SFT+RL > SFT.
knowledges.label: training_recipe_claim

knowledges.content: Figure 6: sampling multiple verification trajectories per candidate (1, 4, 8) monotonically improves Best-of-32 accuracy on MATH500 and Gaokao2023.
knowledges.label: inference_scaling_setting

knowledges.content: Table 3: scaling Agentic Verifier from Qwen3-0.6B to 1.7B to 4B yields monotone accuracy gains (e.g., +5.2 points on Gaokao2023 from 0.6B to 1.7B, with further gains at 4B) on MATH500 and Gaokao2023 across N in {32, 64, 128}.
knowledges.label: model_size_setting

knowledges.content: Best-of-32 accuracy on MATH500 and Gaokao2023 increases as more verification trajectories are sampled (1 -> 4 -> 8).
knowledges.label: inference_scaling_obs

knowledges.content: BoN accuracy on MATH500 and Gaokao2023 increases monotonically as the verifier scales from 0.6B to 1.7B to 4B parameters.
knowledges.label: model_size_obs

knowledges.content: The Agentic Verifier framework scales positively along both axes available to it: inference-time compute (sampled trajectories) and model size (Qwen3 family).
knowledges.label: scaling_law_claim

knowledges.content: Table 4: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B achieves 70.86 on LiveCodeBench and 66.00 on HotpotQA, outperforming Qwen3-4B (57.14 / 40.00), Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, DS-Distill-14B, and Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct.
knowledges.label: generalization_setting

knowledges.content: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B beats all listed baselines on both LiveCodeBench (competitive code) and HotpotQA (multi-hop QA) in Table 4.
knowledges.label: generalization_observation

metadata.prior_justification: Direct read-off from Table 4: Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B leads on both LiveCodeBench and HotpotQA against all listed baselines.

knowledges.content: The bidirectional, tool-augmented agentic-verification paradigm generalizes beyond math to competitive code and multi-hop QA reasoning.
knowledges.label: generalization_claim

knowledges.content: Table 5 latency analysis on a single A100 GPU (vLLM, batch=128) reports Base = 119.0s (2560 tokens, 1 round), Forward = 159.1s (4114 tokens, 5.7 rounds), Backward = 164.3s (4235 tokens, 5.6 rounds), Agentic-Verifier = 323.4s (8349 tokens, 11.3 rounds, 1.6 tool calls).
knowledges.label: latency_setting

knowledges.content: Agentic Verifier consumes ~3.3x more tokens, ~11x more rounds, and ~2.7x more wall clock time than the base verifier; forward-only and backward-only variants sit between the two extremes.
knowledges.label: latency_observation

metadata.prior_justification: Direct read-off from Table 5; numbers are reported with explicit hardware and software configuration (A100 + vLLM, batch=128).

knowledges.content: The accuracy gains of Agentic Verifier come at a substantial computational cost (tokens, rounds, latency); forward-only and backward-only variants offer scalable trade-offs for resource-constrained deployment.
knowledges.label: cost_accuracy_tradeoff_claim

knowledges.content: The reported Table 5 numbers are accurate descriptions of the measured cost.
knowledges.label: latency_axiom

metadata.prior_justification: Author-reported measurements taken as accurate; this serves as the substituted premise in the textbook-style deduction over Table 5.

knowledges.content: Agentic Verifier is a promising paradigm for agentic reward modeling, delivering substantial improvements over SOTA ORMs and PRMs in both parallel and sequential TTS.
knowledges.label: agentic_verifier_promising_claim

knowledges.content: The paper acknowledges three limitations: (1) reliance on synthetic tool-augmented data may not represent real-world reasoning variety, (2) the multi-turn process increases computational cost, and (3) performance is tied to the coverage and reliability of available external tools.
knowledges.label: limitations_setting

knowledges.content: Reliance on synthetic verification trajectories may limit generalization to real-world reasoning problems whose distribution is not covered by the synthetic engine.
knowledges.label: synthetic_data_limit_claim

knowledges.content: The multi-turn deliberative process increases compute cost and presents challenges for real-time or resource-limited deployment.
knowledges.label: cost_limit_claim

knowledges.content: Performance depends on the coverage and reliability of external tools, which can bottleneck certain tasks.
knowledges.label: tool_dependency_limit_claim

knowledges.content: The authors note in the abstract that code is available at GitHub, supporting reproducibility of the AgentV-RL recipe.
knowledges.label: code_release_setting

module_titles.motivation: Introduction and Motivation: Limitations of existing reward models for test-time scaling.
module_titles.s2_related: Section 2: Related Work on reward models and test-time scaling.
module_titles.s3_method: Section 3: Method - Agentic Verifier framework and AgentV-RL training recipe.
module_titles.s4_analysis: Section 4.3: Ablations, scaling, generalization, and latency analysis.
module_titles.s4_experiments: Section 4.2: Main experimental results - parallel and sequential test-time scaling.

metadata.reason: Table 1 shows Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B's headline numbers strictly above the best ORM on every benchmark; the two claims cannot both hold.
Error propagation plus the absence of external grounding within a single-turn reasoning pipeline jointly undermine GenRM reliability.
If verifiers are critical to TTS but current GenRMs are unreliable, the natural remedy is multi-turn, tool-augmented agentic verification.
Prior work either lacks tight tool integration or lacks point-wise feedback; no prior reward model casts verification as bidirectional + multi-turn + tool-augmented.
Sufficiency + necessity together cover both directions of logical entailment, instantiating the agentic paradigm.
A single LLM trained on synthetic forward/backward trajectories with tool use can reproduce the multi-agent behavior pattern.

Standard PPO/GRPO theory: clipping bounds the policy update; zero-variance filtering removes degenerate gradients; KL anchors the policy to a reference. Substituting these into the objective yields a stable update by construction.
The Table 1 ranking, taken with the bidirectional + tool-augmented design argument, attributes the SOTA gap to the proposed method.
Reading the @table1_observation rows for N in {32, 64, 128} shows monotone or near-monotone gains for the 4B variant, especially on AIME24 (40.0 -> 50.0 -> 53.3).

Table 2's high Delta-up / low Delta-down for Agentic-Verifier-Qwen3-4B and its stable performance across turns evidence the helpfulness claim; the multi-turn tool-augmented design explains the robustness against degradation.
Figure 4 directly demonstrates that the union outperforms each component.
No-tool agentic variant beats the base model (agentic gain); enabling tools adds further improvement (tool gain).
Figure 5 quantifies stepwise gains across Train-free / SFT / SFT+RL settings.
If the scaling law holds, sampling more verification trajectories should help.
If the scaling law holds, larger backbone models should help.
Two independent scaling axes (inference compute and model size) both confirm the law.
Two out-of-domain benchmarks show consistent gains, attributed to the same bidirectional + tool design.
Substituting the Table 5 measurements directly establishes the cost differences and the existence of intermediate variants as scalable trade-offs.
Five converging lines of evidence (BoN SOTA, iterative-refinement gains, ablation synergy, scaling, and out-of-domain generalization) all point to the same conclusion.
Table 5's measured ~2.7x latency overhead directly evidences the cost concern.
